text: Pre-event tapering can increase maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max), but this increase is not a necessary prerequisite for taper-induced performance gains.
Submaximal oxygen uptake may decrease during the taper, but this response is more likely in less-skilled athletes than in highly trained ones.
Resting, maximal, and submaximal heart rates do not change during the taper unless the athlete was in a state of overreaching before the taper began.

Haematological adaptations during tapering include increased blood volume, red cell volume, haemoglobin, and haematocrit, reflecting a positive balance between haemolysis and erythropoiesis that likely contributes to performance gains.
Muscle glycogen concentration increases progressively during the taper, while reduced blood creatine kinase concentrations indicate recovery from training-induced muscle damage.

The taper typically results in markedly increased muscular strength and power, often associated with performance gains at both the muscular and whole-body level, along with positive changes in single muscle fibre size and contractile properties.

Psychological benefits of tapering include reduced total mood disturbance, improved somatic relaxation, reduced perception of effort, and improved sleep quality, all of which are associated with improved post-taper performance.

Mathematical modeling indicates taper-induced physiological changes result from restoration of previously impaired capacities (fatigue-adaptation model) and the athlete's capacity to tolerate and respond to training during the taper (variable dose-response model).
